interactions.summary: A student asks whether equilibrium means war stabilizes status competition inside countries; Jiang answers that equilibrium is a balance point where things stay the same.
interactions.speaker_attribution: Student question followed by Jiang answer.

interactions.summary: Jiang carries the equilibrium pattern into Mesopotamia, Persia, and Greece: temple rules preserve elite status, a rule-breaker captures locked-up wealth, empires stagnate, and borderlands with energy, openness, and cohesion defeat mass, organization, and death.

speaker_notes.note: SPEAKER_01 is a student asking and acknowledging a clarification.

claims.claim: Equilibrium means a balance point where things stay the same and participants assume they will stay the same forever.
Jiang interprets Mesopotamian temple immunity as an equilibrium rule: war is allowed, but temple wealth cannot be touched, so expansion is constrained.
He says the Greek-Persian encounter follows the borderland pattern: Greeks learn Persian tactics as mercenaries, gain wealth through piracy, provoke invasion, and then defeat a much larger empire.
He defines empire advantages as mass, organization, and death, while borderlands counter with energy, openness, and cohesion.

signature_moments.moment: Temple wealth is protected by divine fear until a rule-breaker discovers the gods do not strike him down.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Screw this system. I'm going to go for it all
signature_moments.why_it_matters: The Lugazagi story dramatizes how an equilibrium collapses when someone violates the sacred rule keeping wealth locked up.

signature_moments.moment: Empire has mass, organization, and death; the borderland answers with energy, openness, and cohesion.
signature_moments.source_phrase: Mass, organization, and death... energy, openness, and cohesion
signature_moments.why_it_matters: This paired formula becomes the lecture’s portable model for Qin, Greece, Macedonia, and later Rome.

glossary_terms.definition_or_usage: Poor or marginal peoples labeled barbarians who can defeat empires through energy, openness, and cohesion.
Jiang’s triad for empire: population scale, bureaucracy, and willingness to spend lives repeatedly.
Jiang’s counter-triad for borderlands: hard work, learning from others, and social unity.

chronology_notes.note: The lecture presents Mesopotamia, Persia, and Greece as iterations of the same equilibrium-to-borderland conquest model.

uncertainty_notes.note: ASR names Lugazagi/Umma/Sargon/Zagros imperfectly, but the sequence of temple-rule violation, Akkadian empire, and Persian borderland is intelligible.
